title: Migrating from GitHub to Codeberg
text:
I have been using GitHub for while now. Both for collaboration and to host the code for my reseach papers replication. GitHub is great, but recently they had a few issues, probably caused by the huge volume created by automated workflow of AI agents.
This is not a big deal for someone like me that uses source control just for research, but I see more and more people complaining about it, especially with how Microsoft handles the platform. Just try to type “GitHub” on YouTube.
There is also the issue of US and big tech in general. And let’s ignore AI training without consent. So I looked for alternatives. There are a few good ones, most notably GitLab, that I am using with Wageningen University. But recently I found out about Codeberg.

title: Migrating from GitHub to Codeberg
text:
Why Codeberg?
Codeberg is an open source alternative to GitHub. It is European (based in Germany) and their mission is to provide a free, ethical, and non-commercial home for Free and Open Source Software (FOSS) projects. It is run by a non-profit organization, which is funded by donations.
Open source, European, and non-profit? What else can you ask for?
This also aligns better with Open Science principles. If you also care about privacy, and ethics, I highly recommend to check it out. I mean if you are already committed to open science for your publications, why not apply the same principle to your infrastructure? You will have no problem switching if you are familiar with GitHub. The platform works almost exactly the same, such as creating new repositories or collaborating with others. You can also migrate your existing repositories from GitHub to Codeberg.

title: Migrating from GitHub to Codeberg
text:
How to migrate repositories
Migrating my repositories was pretty straightforward. If you also want to migrate follow these steps:

Create an account on Codeberg.
On the top right corner, click on the “+” icon and select “New migration”.
Select “GitHub”.
Copy and paste the URL of the GitHub repository you want to migrate.
Click on “Migrate repository”, and you are done!




Migration process


The original repository will stay intact. You can then decide to keep or delete it.

title: Migrating from GitHub to Codeberg
text:
Conclusion
Switching to Codeberg is a small act. If you already publish open-access, share your data, and post replication code, then hosting that code on a non-profit, European, FOSS platform is a logical step. You are not giving anything up, repositories, collaboration, and CI work just as you would expect but you are opting out of the Microsoft ecosystem, and supporting a more ethical and open alternative.
You can find the code for my research replication on Codeberg here.

title: Positron Configuration for Research and Data Science
text: I started learning R using RStudio, which I think is an an excellent IDE. However, when using other languages I started using VSCode and fell in love with how much you can extend and personlize it. However, I didn’t like how R was integrated in VSCode. That’s when I discovered Positron, a fork of VSCode built by Posit that combines what I like of RStudio with the features of Visual Studio Code.

title: Positron Configuration for Research and Data Science
text: I started learning R using RStudio, which I think is an an excellent IDE. However, when using other languages I started using VSCode and fell in love with how much you can extend and personlize it. However, I didn’t like how R was integrated in VSCode. That’s when I discovered Positron, a fork of VSCode built by Posit that combines what I like of RStudio with the features of Visual Studio Code.

title: Positron Configuration for Research and Data Science
text:
Personalizing Positron
Just like VSCode, Positron is highly customizable. Below I share the extensions and settings I use. These are only suggestions and a work in progress, I will keep updating it as I discover new useful stuff.

Extensions

Air for R language support and formatting
Shiny for Shiny app development
Quarto for Quarto documents
Material Icon Theme for better file icons.
Catppuccin Theme for a nice color theme.

In VSCode I was using Rainbow CSV to look at .csv files, in Positron this is not needed as CSV files are rendered as tables by default, but if you are on VSCode-like editors you might be interested in it.


Settings
The settings below are defined in settings.json. In Positron you can access this file by opening the command palette (Ctrl + Shift + P) and searching for “Preferences: Open Settings (JSON)”. You can expand to see the full JSON that you can copy-paste to your settings.json, followed by an explanation of what each setting does.


Full settings.json (click to expand)

{
    // Formatting
    "[r]": {
        "editor.formatOnSave": true,
        "editor.defaultFormatter": "Posit.air-vscode"
    },

    "[quarto]": {
        "editor.formatOnSave": true,
        "editor.defaultFormatter": "quarto.quarto"
    },

    // Editor

    // Don't open a welcome tab on launch
    "workbench.startupEditor": "none",

    // Show a vertical ruler at 80 characters
    "editor.rulers": [80],
 
    // Custom template for the title bar
    "window.title": "${activeEditorShort}${separator}${rootName}${separator}${profileName}${separator}${activeRepositoryBranchName}",

    // Ignore trailing whitespace in diffs
    "diffEditor.ignoreTrimWhitespace": true,

    // Theme
   
    "workbench.iconTheme": "material-icon-theme",
    "workbench.colorTheme": "Catppuccin Macchiato",

    // Git

    // Automatically fetch from remote repositories
    "git.autofetch": true,

    // Terminal

    // Use Git Bash as the default terminal in windows, and bash in linux
    "terminal.integrated.defaultProfile.windows": "Git Bash",
    "terminal.integrated.defaultProfile.linux": "bash",

    // Disable shell integration to have the terminal run faster
    "terminal.integrated.shellIntegration.enabled": false

    // Misc 

    // Use RStudio-style keybindings as the baseline
    "workbench.keybindings.rstudioKeybindings": true,

    // Enable Positron's built-in AI assistant
    "positron.assistant.enable": true,

    // Preview SVGs as rendered images
    "svg.preview.mode": "svg",

    // Collapse summary statistics panel by default
    "dataExplorer.summaryCollapsed": true,

    "files.associations": {
        "renv.lock": "json"
    },
}

Formatting

editor.formatOnSave + editor.defaultFormatter — Auto-formats R files on save using the Air formatter, and Quarto documents using the Quarto extension’s built-in formatter. This keeps code consistently formatted.

Editor

"workbench.startupEditor": "none" — Disables the welcome/start tab.
"editor.rulers": [80] — Shows a vertical guide line at 80 characters.
"window.title": "${activeEditorShort}${separator}${rootName}${separator}${profileName}${separator}${activeRepositoryBranchName}" — Customizes the title bar to show: active file → project name → profile name → current git branch.
"diffEditor.ignoreTrimWhitespace": true — Ignores trailing whitespace when showing diffs, so changes to indentation don’t clutter the view.

Themes
There are tons of themes and icon packs available in the extension marketplace, these are the ones I use:

"workbench.iconTheme": "material-icon-theme" — Switches the file explorer icons to the Material Icon Theme.
"workbench.colorTheme": "Catppuccin Macchiato" — Sets the color theme to Catppuccin Macchiato.

Git

"git.autofetch": true — Automatically fetches from remote repositories in the background.

Terminal
I use both Windows and Linux machines, so I set the default terminal to Git Bash on Windows and Bash on Linux. Additionally, I disable shell integration for a faster terminal startup (at the cost of some shell-aware features like command decorations):

"terminal.integrated.defaultProfile.windows": "Git Bash" — Uses Git Bash as the default terminal on Windows.
"terminal.integrated.defaultProfile.linux": "bash" — Uses Bash as the default terminal on Linux.
"terminal.integrated.shellIntegration.enabled": false — Disables shell integration for a faster terminal startup (at the cost of some shell-aware features like command decorations).

Other Settings

"workbench.keybindings.rstudioKeybindings": true — Enables RStudio-style keybindings as the starting point. See the Keyboard Shortcuts section below for additional bindings on top of these.
"positron.assistant.enable": true — Enables Positron’s built-in AI assistant.
"svg.preview.mode": "svg" — Previews SVG files as rendered images rather than raw XML.
"dataExplorer.summaryCollapsed": true — Positron has a very nice feature that summarizes the statistics of a dataset when you open it in the data explorer, by default this is always open, I prefer to have it collapsed to give more room to the data table.
"files.associations": { "renv.lock": "json" } — Treats renv.lock as JSON so the editor applies JSON syntax highlighting to it.



Keyboard Shortcuts
In the settings we enabled the RStudio keybindings (workbench.keybindings.rstudioKeybindings: true). The other default keybinds (from VSCode) are stored in “Default Keyboard Shortcuts”. You can define additional custom keybindings in keybindings.json. Again, from the command palette (Ctrl + Shift + P) search “Preferences: Open Keyboard Shortcuts (JSON)”. Below are some of the keybindings I like to use, many are default from VSCode and RStudio.


Full settings.json (click to expand)

// Place your key bindings in this file to override the defaults
[
    { "key": "ctrl+alt+b",
    "command": "workbench.action.toggleAuxiliaryBar" },
    { "key": "ctrl+2",
    "command": "workbench.action.positronConsole.focusConsole" },
]

Sidebar

Ctrl + B Toggle the primary sidebar (activity bar) on/off
Ctrl + Alt + B Toggle the secondary sidebar (auxiliary bar) on/off

Navigation

Ctrl + ` Focus on the Terminal panel
Ctrl + 1 Focus on the editor
Ctrl + 2 Focus on the console
Ctrl + Shift + E Focus on the Explorer view in the sidebar
Ctrl + Shift + G Focus on the Source Control view in the sidebar



Useful resources and inspiration for my configuration:

Heiss, Andrew. 2026. “How to Make Your Data Analysis Life Easier Using Positron, Raycast, and Espanso.” January 13, 2026. https://doi.org/10.59350/zj8p7-7kg49.
Emil Hvitfeldt. “Positron: My Extensions and Settings and “Positron: My Key Bindings”.

title: Riccardo Spada
text:
I’m a PhD Candidate in the Business Economics Group at Wageningen University & Research.
My research focuses on agricultural economics and risk management. I’m passionate about data and personal finance.
